words: SAM SCHRAGER: This fourth interview with Arthur Sundberg is recorded at his
home in Potlatch on August 1, 1975.

speaker: SS
words: First thing that I was thinking of asking you about was the question of

speaker: SS
words: working in the mill. You said to me once that it was, "easy hire; easy

speaker: SS
words: fire".

speaker: AS
words: Each department in the mill, that is in the whole mill complex, it was

speaker: AS
words: divided up into quite a number of different departments, and they had a

speaker: AS
words: foreman in each department and that foreman he hired his crew and he

speaker: AS
words: fired his own crew. That's the way it used to be. And there wasn't any

speaker: AS
words: unions or there was no recourse,if you was fired, you was fired. And

speaker: AS
words: that's it! Nobody ever thought about going on up, you know, to top mana

speaker: AS
words: gement to complain or anything, because that was— I never even heard

speaker: AS
words: tell of such a thing as that you know. They just took it as a matter of

speaker: AS
words: course, you know. They'd hire you right on the spot without any inter

speaker: AS
words: views, they never well, it was customary for a person, particularly

speaker: AS
words: a young person asking for a job, the foreman would kind of eye him up and

speaker: AS
words: down and see how big he was and how strong he appeared to be and if it

speaker: AS
words: was a job working with machinery, the foreman'd probably say, "That guy

speaker: AS
words: right over there, you go over, and he'll show you what to do." So he'd

speaker: AS
words: go over there and that fellow he would — he'd tell that guy, "Well, the

speaker: AS
words: boss sent me over here, you're supposed to show me what to do." And so

speaker: AS
words: he'd probably handle a few boards and, "You do it like this." And prob

speaker: AS
words: ably let you try it there for a minute or two and if it looked like you

speaker: AS
words: do it, he'd just walk off. That was your breaking in period, and

speaker: AS
words: it was just up to you to do it! And there was undoubtedly some, you know,

speaker: AS
words: that had previous experience or at least had previous experience of be

speaker: AS
words: ing in the department, even though they didn't work there, because they

speaker: AS
words: didn't have any restrictions on visitors. Everybody and anybody, kids

speaker: AS
words: or anybody else could just come and go around the mill there. Nobody

speaker: AS
words: ever asked 'em any questions as to what they were doing there, so con

speaker: AS
words: sequently kids that lived in and around that industry, they got quite

speaker: AS
words: a bit of firsthand knowledge of what it was all about as they were grow

speaker: AS
words: ing up. I know kids that'd be seven eight years old or probably young

speaker: AS
words: er than that, they were running all over the plant down there. Kids ten

speaker: AS
words: or eleven years old that were running all over the logs on the log pond,

speaker: AS
words: just trying to stump one another, running on the logs, so the kids got

speaker: AS
words: quite a background knowledge of the industry before they went to work.

speaker: SS
words: How many people would be in the average sized crew? How many would the

speaker: SS
words: foreman be in charge of?

speaker: AS
words: Well, that would depend a whole lot on what department it was; now then

speaker: AS
words: you take, for instance, you'd start in— well, the log pond had probably

speaker: AS
words: four or five men, and when you get up into the sawmill they probably had

speaker: AS
words: probably forty or fifty

speaker: AS
words: For a single foreman?

speaker: AS
words: Yeah. And then get out on the green chain, why, oh, there would be prob

speaker: AS
words: ably twenty men there. Ten men on each side of this chain pulling lum

speaker: AS
words: ber. Approximately that. Well then, in the days before they had the dry

speaker: AS
words: kiln.the lumber used to go from the green chain directly to the lumber

speaker: AS
words: yard on these narrow gauge railroad cars. They had, oh, let's see, I

speaker: AS
words: think they had eight electric locomotives and these locomotives would

speaker: AS
words: haul green lumber— they would haul about six or eight big units of lum

speaker: AS
words: ber at a time. They were coupled together by two and a half inch extra

speaker: AS
words: heavy pipe, and it had a flat coupler on the end. And each one of these

speaker: AS
words: cars had a castiron or caststeel drawhead on each end of the car— they

speaker: AS
words: would put this pipe into that drawhead and then the man had to drop a

speaker: AS
words: pin in that hole. Well, these bars were twenty feet long, so they were

speaker: AS
words: pretty heavy. Well, in making up a train there was a motorman and a

speaker: AS
words: switchman on each one of the locomotives and they carried these bars on

speaker: AS
words: the side of the locomotive on hangers. They would have to take one of

speaker: AS
words: these bars, each one of 'em, and they'd go down along the string of cars

speaker: AS
words: that had to be pulled out, and they would stick one end of the bar into

speaker: AS
words: this coupler and drop a pin in it and then the other end laid on the ground

speaker: AS
words: and they would do that for each car, as many as they was gonna haul. Well

speaker: AS
words: then, the motorman, the motor operator, he would move ahead a little bit

speaker: AS
words: and then the switchman he would have to lay on the side of the track with

speaker: AS
words: this— he held the end of this bar on his leg, you see, and he would have

speaker: AS
words: to gauge that so that it would couple into this drawhead on this car as

speaker: AS
words: he backed up on it, then he dropped the pin in, then jump out and go to

speaker: AS
words: the next one. And that's the way they made up their train. Well then,

speaker: AS
words: they would take it out to the lumberyard. And at that time, in the early

speaker: AS
words: days here, there was about twenty-six or twenty-eight alleys in the lumber

speaker: AS
words: yard, after that it was made larger, but to begin with— and then

speaker: AS
words: there was double tracks in each alley, and these alleys were about eight

speaker: AS
words: een hundred feet long, something like that. Well then, this motor crew

speaker: AS
words: then— say this tailend unit of lumber.it had to go into alley 4East.

speaker: AS
words: Well, they'd L the switch and shove it into alley 4 East and uncouple

speaker: AS
words: it and take the bar out and hang the bar back on the 'locy. The next

speaker: AS
words: unit had to go to alley 6 West, so they'd have to switch over and go down

speaker: AS
words: into alley 6 West and drop it off. Maybe there'd be two of 'em to drop

speaker: AS
words: off. And that's the way they used to distribute 'em. And then it got

speaker: AS
words: into that section of the alley, well then, they had a man out there with

speaker: AS
words: a horse and he would hook his chain onto that car and he would pull that

speaker: AS
words: down and spot it in front of this pile where it was supposed to be piled.

speaker: AS
words: And then the piling crew, they might have to just push it by hand one way

speaker: AS
words: or the other to just get it spotted right, and then they would pile it.

speaker: AS
words: Well then— I think there were about five locomotives pulling green lum

speaker: AS
words: ber and there was three, I think it was, that was pulling dry lumber,

speaker: AS
words: but these locomotives that pulled the dry lumber in, they would haul six

speaker: AS
words: or eight united of green lumber— the same size locomotive hauling dry

speaker: AS
words: lumber, they would haul eleven, twelve, maybe fourteen units of dry lumber

speaker: AS
words: back in. Well, what they done then when they brought it they

speaker: AS
words: would pull it up behind the planer and there was several tracks, there

speaker: AS
words: was a regular switchyard there, and they would just uncouple all of their

speaker: AS
words: units right there and stack the bars up on the 'locy and then go back for

speaker: AS
words: another drag. But, I think I told you the planer, it had twenty-two mach

speaker: AS
words: ines in the planer, and there was a track going into each machine. And

speaker: AS
words: then there was two tracks going out from each machine; one track was for

speaker: AS
words: the lumber that was on grade for that particular order and the other track

speaker: AS
words: was for, what they called, outs. You know, that was the lumber that was

speaker: AS
words: either higher or lower grade than what was called for and then that unit

speaker: AS
words: would be pulled out and go to the lumbershed for stacking up.

speaker: SS
words: Then the lumber went through the planer just when it was on order?

speaker: AS
words: Yeah, that's right. It all stayed in the lumberyard rough, except, as I

speaker: AS
words: say, the outs, that went to the lumbershed, and of course, they'd had an

speaker: AS
words: order for a certain specie and a certain grade and dimension, well, then,

speaker: AS
words: whatever they could of that order, they filled that out of the shed,that

speaker: AS
words: was already surfaced. And then the remainder come from the lumberyard

speaker: AS
words: to be surfaced. But, oh, in the early years, they didn't do any remanufacturing.

speaker: AS
words: The only remanufacturing that was done with the surfaced lum

speaker: AS
words: ber was that this lumber that come into the lumbershed for stacking up,

speaker: AS
words: well, it was all graded again in the shed and then it was stacked in dif

speaker: AS
words: ferent units in this shed for dimension and grade and so on. But if

speaker: AS
words: they had say, a number three board and it was a number three because it

speaker: AS
words: had a two foot crack on one end of the board, you know, a check, that

speaker: AS
words: made it a number three board, where otherwise if that wasn't there it

speaker: AS
words: could be a number one board. Well, maybe the man that was grading in

speaker: AS
words: the shed; each one of these graders in there had a hand saw and he would

speaker: AS
words: saw off two feet of of the end of it by hand and then stack it up. That

speaker: AS
words: would upgrade the board. But that's the only remanufacturing that was

speaker: AS
words: done in the early days.

speaker: SS
words: You said that the planer mill surfaced the lumber; what do you mean by

speaker: SS
words: that?

speaker: AS
words: Well, it was rough lumber when it come in and then when it come out it

speaker: AS
words: was smooth. You know, it was planed top and bottom and two edges, pos-

speaker: AS
words: sibly. And a lot of the lumber in the early days was just— it was speci

speaker: AS
words: fied as two-side. Well, that meant that it was surfaced on top and bot

speaker: AS
words: tom. And then, of course, you had other orders that was specified as

speaker: AS
words: four-side; that was top and bottom and the two edges, were surfaced.

speaker: AS
words: But there was an awful lot of lumber shipped in the early years, where

speaker: AS
words: the edges were never surfaced at all, I think because of the fact that

speaker: AS
words: there was a lot of that lumber that the purchaser would remanufacture

speaker: AS
words: himself, and he would smooth up the edges. But anyway, there was, oh,

speaker: AS
words: I'd say that it'd be fifty percent of it, or maybe more, that was shipped

speaker: AS
words: just surfaced two sides.

speaker: SS
words: What was the relation between the mill and the WIM Railroad? As far as

speaker: SS
words: the transference of the finished lumber to the railroad for shipment out?

speaker: SS
words: Was this really run as a separate operation?

speaker: AS
words: It was, but it was entirely owned by the lumber company. But then it was

speaker: AS
words: run as a separate industry. And they had their own method of hiring and

speaker: AS
words: and operations, and of course, they had to comply with the Interstate

speaker: AS
words: Commerce Commission rules on everything. But it was owned entirely by

speaker: AS
words: the lumber company, but it was run as an entirely separate Industry, see?

speaker: SS
words: These foremen; if you wanted a job in the mill, you would go down there

speaker: SS
words: and speak to the foreman in the area where you thought you might want to

speaker: SS
words: work?

speaker: AS
words: That's right. You would go to whatever department that you thought you

speaker: AS
words: would like to work. Now, for instance out in the lumberyard, they had

speaker: AS
words: a foreman for the green lumber pilers, and then they had another foreman

speaker: AS
words: for the men that loaded dry lumber, loaded out. And then they had an

speaker: AS
words: other foreman for the pick-up. Now that pick-up was, it was probably,

speaker: AS
words: oh, anywhere from two or three to five or six men in that pick-up crew,

speaker: AS
words: but they had a lead man that was called a foreman. And what they done

speaker: AS
words: was; any lumber that was— had fallen off or was scattered, and. all these

speaker: AS
words: cross pieces that was in the pile you know— each course of lumber that

speaker: AS
words: stacked, it had these one-by-four cross pieces on there so that it

speaker: AS
words: would make .circulation through the stack. Well, this dry lumber crew,

speaker: AS
words: that was loading out; it wasn't too long after the mill started where

speaker: AS
words: that went on piecework, on gyppo, you see? ^Got so much a thousand for

speaker: AS
words: loading it out. So what they would do, they'd just throw them one-byfours

speaker: AS
words: off the pile to get 'em out of their road, see? Well, this pick

speaker: AS
words: up crew, they would go up and down the alleys and see that those were all

speaker: AS
words: put back up to where they wasn't laying on the rails. Just kind of a

speaker: AS
words: housekeeping chore. Well, that was another department. A man might go

speaker: AS
words: out in the lumberyard and ask the dry lumber foreman for a job, and he'd

speaker: AS
words: say, "Well, right now I'm filled up, I just can't hire anybody right now.

speaker: AS
words: But, Old Carl, over there - go over and see him, maybe he needs a man."

speaker: AS
words: So, go over and see Carl, he was on pick-up crew, and "Yeah, I can

speaker: AS
words: put you on. You start in right away." And you'd work at that for a

speaker: AS
words: while, but then after, or maybe in a short while, there'd be an opening

speaker: AS
words: in this— where you wanted to start in the first place in this dry lumber

speaker: AS
words: crew, so then, you would— they needed a man there, so you would go over

speaker: AS
words: there and go to work. Then Old Carl, he'd hire another man in his pick

speaker: AS
words: up crew. But fcach one, they hired their own crew, and, as I say, if it

speaker: AS
words: was necessary,would fire.

speaker: SS
words: WAs it too hard to move from job to job, once you were working in the

speaker: SS
words: mill?

speaker: AS
words: No, I don't think so. Of course, you had to go and talk to that other

speaker: AS
words: foreman, wherever you wanted to go, and he'd say, "Well, where you work

speaker: AS
words: ing now?" "Well, I'm working up in the sawmill." "Well, what's the mat

speaker: AS
words: ter with that?" he'd say. "Well, it's just too noisy, I just want to get

speaker: AS
words: out of there." And he might then, the first opportunity, ask the saw

speaker: AS
words: mill foreman about it, and say, "Well, so-and-so asked me for a job, what

speaker: AS
words: do you think?" "Oh, go ahead and hire him, he's no good here anyway."

speaker: AS
words: And, that's the way it'd go, see? (Chuckles)

speaker: SS
words: Then he would hire him, would he?

speaker: AS
words: Oh, yeah. But he might be good in that other because that's where

speaker: AS
words: he wanted to be. Well, it was a far cry from the way it's operated now-adays,

speaker: AS
words: you know. Now-a-days you firstJhave to go to the employment office

speaker: AS
words: and there you have to be interviewed as to past experience and all that

speaker: AS
words: and then they might give you an aptitude test as to— if they think you

speaker: AS
words: can put a round peg in a square hole or a square peg in a round hole!!

speaker: AS
words: And then, of course, now it's very seldom that you go directly into

speaker: AS
words: a department or a division of the plant that you would like to go into

speaker: AS
words: right at the beginning, because a big percentage of the new hires now-a

speaker: AS
words: days goes on a extra board list, and you're called out to day shift or

speaker: AS
words: night shift and maybe one day you'll work in the power plant and the next

speaker: AS
words: day maybe they'll call you out to work in the planing mill, and so if

speaker: AS
words: you stay with it you'll finally get to where you want to go.

speaker: SS
words: Would fellows want to move around very much in the old days, or would

speaker: SS
words: a guy stay for years?

speaker: AS
words: No, the fact of the matter is; you take the average person working in the

speaker: AS
words: lumber industry, my opinion is, that it was just a matter of a job, a mat

speaker: AS
words: ter of making a living for himself and his family, if he had one, and

speaker: AS
words: they would go through an awful lot of hardships just in order to keep

speaker: AS
words: their job and to be able to provide a living. More so than anybody nowa-

speaker: AS
words: days. You take anyone your age, I don't think that the person could

speaker: AS
words: paint a picture and really get the idea across as to what people actually

speaker: AS
words: had to go through. I say, had to go through, that's probably a good way

speaker: AS
words: to put it, but that was just the way things were done and if you was go

speaker: AS
words: ing to survive you had to do it, that was all. Now you take now-a-days

speaker: AS
words: you see these young people, the young men,- I know I used to get so dis

speaker: AS
words: gusted with some of 'em— they'd come down to work in the morning and

speaker: AS
words: they'd have on a pair of jeans,  underwear, they'd probably just a T

speaker: AS
words: shirt and shorts, and a little low pair of socks and a pair

speaker: AS
words: of oxfords, and they'd come down there and it'd be below zero!! And

speaker: AS
words: there they'd stand and shiver and shake, just like that. Well, you know

speaker: AS
words: no man can work dressed like that. You have to be comfortable if you're

speaker: AS
words: going to work!! And, you know, in my crew, of course, my crew was maint

speaker: AS
words: enance and construction work, but I had men in my crew that would come

speaker: AS
words: down dressed that way and I used to tie into 'em and ask 'em why in the

speaker: AS
words: world they didn't buy some clothes, and they'd mumble something. But

speaker: AS
words: then I always kept a lot of my clothes Inhere; I had clothes enough for

speaker: AS
words: half a dozen men of my own. You know, sheep skin lined overcoats and

speaker: AS
words: all kinds of rain gear; pants and coats and overshoes and everything else.

speaker: AS
words: Well, gee whiz, them guys were wearing my clothes all the time.

speaker: SS
words: We were talking about back then, we were talking about what they had to

speaker: SS
words: put up with.

speaker: AS
words: Back then they used to dress, they knew they had to, and if they didn't you

speaker: AS
words: know a foreman, if the fellow had the audicity to come down on the

speaker: AS
words: job in the early days like they do now, dressed the way they do why the

speaker: AS
words: foreman wouldn't give one look, and if he was already employed, he'd can

speaker: AS
words: him right on the spot!! They wouldn't put up with anything like that.

speaker: AS
words: Them days, you take in the cold weather, there was any number of differ

speaker: AS
words: ent combinations of footwear that the men used to wear trying to keep

speaker: AS
words: their feet reasonably warm, but there was a lot of 'em that wore what

speaker: AS
words: they called German socks. Well they were just a felt boot, you know,

speaker: AS
words: they were about so thick and they'd have on wool socks and then they'd

speaker: AS
words: have this thing on and then they'd have overshoes over the top of that.

speaker: AS
words: But, I guess personally I've tried every kind of.combination that's ever

speaker: AS
words: been thought of for cold weather and the best thing that I ever found

speaker: AS
words: was just a pair of low rubbers, 'course they used to make 'em special for

speaker: AS
words: that, they don't make 'em any more like that, but they were heavy, you

speaker: AS
words: know but pliable and then have maybe one or two felt liners, you know,

speaker: AS
words: insoles in there, and then maybe a pair of light socks then a pair of

speaker: AS
words: heavy woold socks on that. Well that was pliable and flexible, you see.

speaker: AS
words: Anthing that's stiff on your feet, you'd freeze your feet, because your

speaker: AS
words: feet wouldn't have a chance to flex . And then the same with your hands.

speaker: AS
words: I don't know whether I was among the minority, but I figured it out for

speaker: AS
words: myself.- Long time ago it was customary for men to wear a pair of wool

speaker: AS
words: mittens and the wool mittens would be inside of a pair of leather mittens.

speaker: AS
words: Well, I found out for myself, and I put a good many other men wise to it

speaker: AS
words: too, and they followed my theory, was that you could freeze your hands in

speaker: AS
words: that combination because inside of this wool mitten you had skin against

speaker: AS
words: skin on your fingers there, see? Where you could just wear a cotton glove

speaker: AS
words: inside of that wool mitten and stay warm, because you had insulation between

speaker: AS
words: your fingers, you see.A Even right now you go around in the winter

speaker: AS
words: time and you'll see these guys, oh they got to get their hands warm, so

speaker: AS
words: they go and buy a pair of wool gloves and then a pair of leather gloves,

speaker: AS
words: you know, they got to have a leather glove, mitten to handle lumber with.

speaker: AS
words: But, their hands are cold, and it's just because your skin is coming in

speaker: AS
words: contact with the skin on the other finger, where if you had some insul

speaker: AS
words: ation between thd^fingers, then you would stay warm.

speaker: SS
words: How tough was it for these guys in the crews with the foreman? I've heard

speaker: SS
words: about brownnosing, for example—

speaker: AS
words: Well, that's true. You would probably find, in some crews, you know,

speaker: AS
words: might be one or two in the crew that was always brownnosing around the

speaker: AS
words: boss, you know. Trying to win favors. Every chance they got they

speaker: AS
words: would slip around the boss, "John Doe over there, he done this or he

speaker: AS
words: done that." Trying to get that guy in trouble and make a little higher

speaker: AS
words: in the esteem, of the boss. But I wouldn t say that that was the pre

speaker: AS
words: vailing thing. It was— you'd find that maybe with some and then some

speaker: AS
words: that wouldn't work at all, you know. Maybe there was some foreman that

speaker: AS
words: just wouldn't put up with that kind of stuff. If there was any investigations

speaker: AS
words: as to anything that wasn't supposed to be, he'd^find that out

speaker: AS
words: for himself. But then, there was some foremen that, well, they lacked

speaker: AS
words: the intelligence and the experience of a foreman, so they relied on some

speaker: AS
words: of these other guys bringing stuff to 'em, you see? You know, if a per

speaker: AS
words: son doesn't have the qualifications for a foreman, well then, he might

speaker: AS
words: have to resort to some of that stuff. And then, there was some fore

speaker: AS
words: men that done an awful lot of brownnosing to the superintendent, too,

speaker: AS
words: you know. And of course, that's : as far up as they could go, they

speaker: AS
words: never got up to the management top; top management.

speaker: SS
words: Would that be making himself look good at the expense of his crew?

speaker: AS
words: Oh, yeah, yeah. Or somebody else's crew. Now there's been instances

speaker: AS
words: where one foreman of say a small crew, he would try to get the idea over

speaker: AS
words: to the superintendent that he could run his own crew and your crew! Com

speaker: AS
words: bine the two together; he could run it all. And maybe if that was done,

speaker: AS
words: maybe it wouldn't be too long until he'd want to take in another crew.

speaker: AS
words: Well, that would keep snowballing and pretty soon he'd be a superintend

speaker: AS
words: ent, wouldn't he? 'Cause he'd be running the whole show!! And there

speaker: AS
words: was men that had those ambitions. 'Course most of 'em was kinda picked

speaker: AS
words: in the bud, they didn't get too far. They did multiply a little bit,

speaker: AS
words: but they always stopped it before it got too far along. If a man had a

speaker: AS
words: crew here maybe of ten men, well, maybe he was capable of combining his

speaker: AS
words: crew with another crew and making it all in one. There was some people

speaker: AS
words: that had the capability of doing that, and then there was probably some

speaker: AS
words: too that wanted to do that and maybe they had an opportunity to do it,

speaker: AS
words: then after they got it they found out they couldn't handle it. Well then,

speaker: AS
words: they would get one of their subordinates to take over part of it, you

speaker: AS
words: see? Part of the load. Oh, there are lots of different combinations

speaker: AS
words: you know that can be worked out.

speaker: SS
words: Sounds like there was a great degree of flexibility in the set up to get

speaker: SS
words: that kind of thing. You could have actually, let's say, subdivide your

speaker: SS
words: crew and the foreman have one of your men run part of the crew. Sounds

speaker: SS
words: like it wasn't too highly structured, is what I'm saying.

speaker: AS
words: Well, that's true, but this subordinate to the foreman, he didn't get any

speaker: AS
words: recognition of being a leader or anything else. That was just between

speaker: AS
words: him and his foreman, you know, and he didn't get any extra pay. It was

speaker: AS
words: just making him look good to his boss, that was all. A glory thing, you

speaker: AS
words: know, he didn't get any extra compensation for it or anything of the kind.

speaker: SS
words: I've heard it said that whether or not you had a good foreman is what

speaker: SS
words: made all the difference.

speaker: AS
words: iust the— well,,, started out with this manager that I told you hated

speaker: AS
words: my guts, started out right there. And I blame him for just fixing it up

speaker: AS
words: so that it'd keep me off from it, you know. So I'm losing, oh, I'm los

speaker: AS
words: ing at least four hundred dollars every month and have been now for over

speaker: AS
words: eleven years.

speaker: SS
words: Was he still in the operation?

speaker: AS
words: No, he was out. You mean the one that didn't like me?

speaker: SS
words: Yeah. He was out when they came up with your pension plan?

speaker: AS
words: No, no, he was here.

speaker: SS
words: He was here?

speaker: AS
words: Oh, yeah..

speaker: SS
words: So you figure that there was a great chance that he had to do with —

speaker: AS
words: At least that's the way I always felt about it. Now I could be wrong,

speaker: AS
words: but then that's the way I always felt.

speaker: SS
words: Were there very few people in that class you were in that were being paid

speaker: SS
words: an hourly wage?

speaker: AS
words: No, I was about the only one. That is, not only in this unit but in the

speaker: AS
words: whole PFI organization.

speaker: SS
words: Everyone else had gone back on a salary?

speaker: AS
words: Yes, although you take long as I was on the job I was making more money

speaker: AS
words: than any of 'em.

speaker: SS
words: That's really too bad.

speaker: AS
words: Well, we'll survive. But.I think for the work that I done for the company,

speaker: AS
words: I don't think I was treated right in that respect, because I think you

speaker: AS
words: can go right down to Lewiston and check with any of the brass down there

speaker: AS
words: and ask what they think about my work, and I think I'd get a pretty good

speaker: AS
words: rating from any of 'em. Top man down.

speaker: SS
words: I want to ask you about the IWW activities we touched on a little bit.

speaker: SS
words: What's your understanding of what their aims were and what they did in

speaker: SS
words: the camps?

speaker: AS
words: Well, I'll tell you, to start out with, I think the^principal aim at the

speaker: AS
words: beginning was to get clean beds and decent food in the camps. In the

speaker: AS
words: time they started, before that— every man had to carry his own bedroll.

speaker: AS
words: They had wooden bunks built in the camps, but then every man would go

speaker: AS
words: down to the barn and get an armful of straw, bing it up and thrown it in

speaker: AS
words: that bunk and spread his own blankets on it, and that was his bed. And

speaker: AS
words: the food— Of course, I can't tell you this from first hand because I

speaker: AS
words: wasn't there—but from all the men that I talked to, was that if the camp

speaker: AS
words: foreman, if he could run his camp at the least cost, he would raise him

speaker: AS
words: self up in the esteem of the company. But if he would raise the cost by

speaker: AS
words: ordering real good, expensive food for the men, why then he didn't stay

speaker: AS
words: there very long as foreman, because it cost too much to run his camp.

speaker: AS
words: They would buy the very best timothy hay and they bought the best grade

speaker: AS
words: of oats and they hired the very best blacksmith to shoe their horses,

speaker: AS
words: and they bought the very best harness and equipment for the^horses, but

speaker: AS
words: when it come to feeding the men, well, they wasn't any of the camps that

speaker: AS
words: had any refrigeration but nevertheless, I know that they was meat that

speaker: AS
words: left this store down here— they wholesaled, you know for all the camps,

speaker: AS
words: meat was shipped out of here that actually maggots were crawling out of

speaker: AS
words: it and they didn't want to cut it up for the meat market here so they

speaker: AS
words: shipped it to the camps.

speaker: AS
words: Well, you know that word gets out and the men

speaker: AS
words: put up an awful howl about that. And I know that in the camps, as well

speaker: AS
words: as in some of the boardinghouses that when the meat got tainted to where

speaker: AS
words: it would actually smell bad, well then the cook would throw in a bunch

speaker: AS
words: of salt petre in the pot to kill that, and of course when you put a piece

speaker: AS
words: of meat on your plate that meat was just as red as it could be from that

speaker: AS
words: salt petre. Whenever you get a piece of red meat after it's cooked, well

speaker: AS
words: that's salt petre. And the men would have dysentary and they'd be lined

speaker: AS
words: up there, just in a big line, you know waiting to get into the John. And

speaker: AS
words: running in every direction as faf*as that was concerned. So the food, in

speaker: AS
words: some cases pretty bad. Well, that's one of the things that the IWWS

speaker: AS
words: was trying to eliminate. They wanted good food and they wanted clean

speaker: AS
words: beds. They didn't object to hard work. They didn't object to working

speaker: AS
words: in the mud or in the deep snow, in the cold or in the heat, they didn't

speaker: AS
words: object to any of that but they wanted— when they come in, they wanted

speaker: AS
words: a good meal and a clean bed, and that's what they fought for. There

speaker: AS
words: wasn't anything wrong with that.

speaker: AS
words: But at the time,

speaker: AS
words: asking for all these things, well then, they were radical men, see? Any

speaker: AS
words: body that would ask for stuff like that; they're radical, they're bol

speaker: AS
words: shevics, you know. They would picture 'em in the cartoons in the news

speaker: AS
words: paper with big horn rimmed glasses and chin whiskers and a bomb in their

speaker: AS
words: hand, you know. That's the way the cartoonists would picture 'em, you

speaker: AS
words: know!! Well, at the same time, that is^the same period of time— the

speaker: AS
words: farmers around the country— there used to be an awful lot of smut in

speaker: AS
words: their wheat, I've the blower on some of these threshing machines, you'd

speaker: AS
words: think they were burning coal, the straw coming out was just a black cloud

speaker: AS
words: of smut. So every once in a while there'd be a threshing machine'd ex

speaker: AS
words: plode and burn. Well, the IWWs done that, see. Well the IWWs didn't

speaker: AS
words: have anything to do with the^farmers, they were up here in the woods.

speaker: AS
words: But nevertheless, in all the newspapers—[you go back into the archives

speaker: AS
words: of some of these newspapers you'd see where this threshing machine and

speaker: AS
words: that wheat field burned and the IWWs done that. And this locomotive

speaker: AS
words: jumped the track and the IWWs done that!! Well, they got blamed for

speaker: AS
words: an awful lot of stuff that I don't think they had anything to do with

speaker: AS
words: at all.

speaker: AS
words: Were the representatives, what you heard, of the IWWs in the woods, were

speaker: AS
words: they just in a lot of cases lumberjacks?

speaker: AS
words: Yes. In all cases. They were men that— well, payday comes— most of

speaker: AS
words: the men would head for Spokane, for the weekend. Get drunk; spend all

speaker: AS
words: their money and come back to camp broke. But while they were in Spokane

speaker: AS
words: or^Seattle or in Portland or wherever they might be, they were around

speaker: AS
words: the lumber industry, why, some of 'em were pickedJas an individual by

speaker: AS
words: organized labor to try to organize the men in the camp. Well, you know,

speaker: AS
words: organized labor for a good many years before that period sure had a rough

speaker: AS
words: row to hoe in the Midwest and in the East, around steelmills and the coal

speaker: AS
words: mines and stuff. You know it developed into a civil war back in that part

speaker: AS
words: of the country, you know between laborers and management. And of course,

speaker: AS
words: some of those representatives got out into this part of the country, and

speaker: AS
words: of course, at that time lumbering was a pretty big thing all through

speaker: AS
words: Wisconsin and Minnesota and then jumped 'way out here. But it started

speaker: AS
words: out some guy probably pulling a crosscut saw in the woods and he'd talk

speaker: AS
words: to his partner and probably convert him and then those two would talk

speaker: AS
words: to two others and so on, and pretty soon, why, they got together to where

speaker: AS
words: they began to make some demands that they was either going to get some

speaker: AS
words: clean beds or they was going to strike and tie up this camp to where you

speaker: AS
words: can't move a log. So what did they do, the management called on the

speaker: AS
words: sheriff. So what does the sheriff do, but he organizes a homeguard. He

speaker: AS
words: deputizes everybody that he can get ahold of, and in some cases they is

speaker: AS
words: sued a whole bunch of 30-30 rifles and boy if the Wobblies come around

speaker: AS
words: here they was gonna shoot 'em, see? But that's the way organized labor

speaker: AS
words: got started..

speaker: SS
words: Well in the camps, I've heard that there were strikes, or at least one

speaker: SS
words: strike in most of the lumber camps out here, that work did stop.

speaker: AS
words: Oh yeah, there was undoubtedly.work stoppages and of course, they wouldn't

speaker: AS
words: stop very long until they'd get 'em going again, and when they did they

speaker: AS
words: had clean beds and they had better working conditions. And then another

speaker: AS
words: h thing too,they're responsible, I think, for the eight-hour day. You

speaker: AS
words: know along with other organized labor because ten hours was the standard

speaker: AS
words: workday all over— in any industry, as far as that was concerned.

speaker: SS
words: Do you know about the bullpen that they had in Moscow? For the IWWs?

speaker: AS
words: Well, I heard of it, I didn't see it but I don't think they ever put any

speaker: AS
words: body in it. Might have throwed a few drunks in there or something like

speaker: AS
words: that, ifwas just something that I know the top management here, we seen

speaker: AS
words: the sheriff over here ever so many times and he'd be up in the office with

speaker: AS
words: the general manager talking things over. And then it got to where the

speaker: AS
words: sheriff then went up on the street and everybody'd come down the street,

speaker: AS
words: every man, why,"Come on over." and he'd deputize 'em.

speaker: SS
words: That's how they dealt with it in Potlatch?

speaker: AS
words: Oh yeah.

speaker: SS
words: The IWW didn't make inroads here?

speaker: AS
words: No, they didn't do anything down here. was all in the logging camps.

speaker: SS
words: What's the militia that was formed here? What's the story on that? I've

speaker: SS
words: been told that that was what they used to keep the IWWs out.

speaker: AS
words: Well, that was formed at that time, but they never had anything to do

speaker: AS
words: with the IWWs. Fact of the matter is, I was a member of that militia

speaker: AS
words: myself. I've got a panoramic picture here someplace about that long,

speaker: AS
words: showing everybody in the militia, you know, in that picture. But it was

speaker: AS
words: something that was sponsored and organized by top management, you know

speaker: AS
words: through the law enforcement officials. And, oh, they were scared, you

speaker: AS
words: know. The management was scared, I think they could see the handwriting

speaker: AS
words: on the wall that labor was determined to organize and there was no stop

speaker: AS
words: ping 'em. And of course, I think even when you got to top management

speaker: AS
words: they had the same picture of a union man. And organized labor is just

speaker: AS
words: a bunch of anarchists, you know, people with bombs and was going to destroy

speaker: AS
words: the whole world. That was the picture of what was to come.

speaker: SS
words: Do you think though that the militia was sort of a disguised threat of

speaker: SS
words: the possibility of what management would want the town to do if the Wobblies

speaker: SS
words: tried to organize?

speaker: AS
words: Well of course, you take— if a man was sworn in as a deputy sheriff and

speaker: AS
words: then if he was called on by the sheriff to do some patrol work or something,

speaker: AS
words: he'd pretty near have to do it. But that doesn't necessarily

speaker: AS
words: mean that he'd have to fire a gun or to shoot at somebody, you know.

speaker: SS
words: Was there any talk that you can remember when you were in the militia

speaker: SS
words: that you might have to defend against the—

speaker: AS
words: Oh, yes, yeah, yeah. There was a lot of talk about that, and the fact

speaker: AS
words: of the matter is, I think possibly I might have been just as gullible

speaker: AS
words: as most of the rest of them, because you take — the Wobblies were some

speaker: AS
words: thing new to all of us, you know, the fact of the matter is, we never

speaker: AS
words: had any personal contact with them. And our knowledge of the Wobblies

speaker: AS
words: was what we read in the newspapers and what we were told by officials.

speaker: AS
words: And there's no reason in the world why a person shouldn't believe what

speaker: AS
words: they read in the newspapers. You know, whatever you read in the newspapers

speaker: AS
words: was gospel truth. There was no such thing in the beliefs of the average

speaker: AS
words: person that the newspapers would ever tell a lie! They published facts!

speaker: AS
words: (Chuckles) That just shows how gullible people are, at least how gullible

speaker: AS
words: they were. Fact of the matter is, you know the average American citizen,

speaker: AS
words: he was just a plain, hard working person that didn't want to bother any

speaker: AS
words: body else and they didn't want to be bothered by somebody else, and all

speaker: AS
words: they wanted to do was work every day and just make a living. And that

speaker: AS
words: was the primary goal of the average American citizen, whether they lived

speaker: AS
words: in New York State or in Idaho.

speaker: SS
words: Do you think that what the IWWs did in the woods had positive effect on

speaker: SS
words: the improvement of conditions in the town of Potlatch?

speaker: AS
words: Oh yeah, you know, that s very true. Because you take when I

speaker: AS
words: organized and of course, they got this eight hour day, well of course,

speaker: AS
words: all the lumber producing mills had to go on an eight hour day, and of

speaker: AS
words: course, I can remember how they used to cry that they was just gonna go

speaker: AS
words: bankrupt, they just couldn't make it, you know, if they was just gonna

speaker: AS
words: work eight hours a day, K management felt that way. But then I guess

speaker: AS
words: they found out when they got to checking all their accounts and their

speaker: AS
words: books, that men would probably produce as much in eight hours as they

speaker: AS
words: did in ten hours, because they weren't as tired, for one thing. I know

speaker: AS
words: just in recent years they—LoUlS Young was the chief engineer down

speaker: AS
words: here at the plant, and of course he was the superintendent of power and

speaker: AS
words: maintenance. He was my superior. Well, they turned the management of

speaker: AS
words: the sawmill over to him, although they had a sawmill foreman, and they

speaker: AS
words: used to always have an extra man that was supposed to relieve these men

speaker: AS
words: on the job— various jobs— because where you're working with machinery

speaker: AS
words: that machinery has to keep going. So, consequently, the last man or two

speaker: AS
words: in the crew, his relief would come at ten minutes before twelve o'clock,

speaker: AS
words: or ten minutes before four o'clock, so what did he do? He'd just take

speaker: AS
words: his hat and go home. He had ten minutes coming and by the time that ten

speaker: AS
words: minutes was up, why the shift was over, see? Because too late getting

speaker: AS
words: around there. So management, they seen this guy going up the hill, going

speaker: AS
words: home, and they seen that for quite a number of times and finally they

speaker: AS
words: began to question as to how come that feller left his job and went home

speaker: AS
words: ten minutes before twelve o'clock. Well then, they tried to explain that

speaker: AS
words: he had this ten minutes relief coming and he didn't get it until ten min

speaker: AS
words: utes,

speaker: AS
words: Well, so Young he come up with the idea that

speaker: AS
words: they'd do away with that relief man entirely. That way they took one

speaker: AS
words: man off the payroll. But, at nine o'clock and at two o'clock they would

speaker: AS
words: shut the whole thing down for ten minutes. That way everybody took their

speaker: AS
words: break. At that time they could smoke or they could go to the John or

speaker: AS
words: they could do whatever they pleased, but they had ten minutes and no more

speaker: AS
words: but before that a man was supposed to have ten minutes, but he took twenty

speaker: AS
words: minutes or he took twenty-five minutes or he took thirty minutes. And

speaker: AS
words: that's the reason that this man didn't get around to get his relief until

speaker: AS
words: ten minutes before quitting time. So when they had this break then at

speaker: AS
words: nine o'clock for ten minutes, and at two o'clock, that's all they got

speaker: AS
words: was ten minutes. So top management, they began to holler then, "Oh, gee

speaker: AS
words: whiz, we can't do that, we can't shut the whole thing down, the whole

speaker: AS
words: plant down for ten minutes. You're gonna lose production." But when

speaker: AS
words: they got to checking the production sheet they actually had higher pro

speaker: AS
words: duction than they had before, when they kept it running. So there was

speaker: AS
words: quite a lot of controversy about that for a while a lot of static, but

speaker: AS
words: it wasn't long until the whole Potlatch organization adopted that plan.

speaker: AS
words: They use that plan in all their plants. And I think if you go to the

speaker: AS
words: industry— lumber industry— all over, you'll find that's

speaker: SS
words: Do you know about when he instituted that change? Roughly.

speaker: AS
words: Oh, he retired in '54 and it was somewhere around 1952 or somewhere around

speaker: AS
words: in there when he put that into effect.

speaker: SS
words: I wanted to ask you about— you know you said that you were probably gul

speaker: SS
words: lible too back when the IWW was fighting to improve the conditions. When

speaker: SS
words: do you think you started to see, or changed your mind about it?

speaker: AS
words: Oh, I think it wasiseveral years afterwards, because, fact of the matter

speaker: AS
words: is— you know the average person really didn't come in contact with any

speaker: AS
words: thing that had anything to do with 'em at all. You know, we read in the

speaker: AS
words: newspapers and of course, that was the only line of communication we had

speaker: AS
words: then.was the newspaper. And so you'd take the newspaper's word for it.

speaker: SS
words: But what would have changed your mine? Did you have a chance to talk to

speaker: SS
words: some lumberjacks who were involved?

speaker: AS
words: Well, you would actually see and talking with people they'd tell you about

speaker: AS
words: how much the conditions had improved. And then of course, you'd start

speaker: AS
words: forming your own opinion as to who should have the credit for this im

speaker: AS
words: provement.

speaker: SS
words: I know that in some of the printed literature that you read, the lumber

speaker: SS
words: companies like to take the credit for the improvements, themselves. To

speaker: SS
words: say that in fact they wouldn't make any of these improvements until they

speaker: SS
words: got rid of the Wobblies, and then they made the improvements. I, myself,

speaker: SS
words: haven't bought that.

speaker: AS
words: Well, I think probably they're true, and again the other side might be

speaker: AS
words: true. Now then, any man that was known to be a member of the IWW, he

speaker: AS
words: was canned. So after they got 'em all weeded out, why then they probably

speaker: AS
words: brought on these improvements, but still, the IWWs wasn't given the credit

speaker: AS
words: for it. You see, the company was given the total credit. But they used

speaker: AS
words: to have secret meetings; everything had to be secret, you know. I attended

speaker: AS
words: one meeting one Sunday, in Spokane. There was a bunch of us up

speaker: AS
words: there just cattin around and so we heard about this Wobbly meeting in the

speaker: AS
words: hall there, and so we went up there, 'course they'd have it on Sunday.

speaker: AS
words: And to listen to some of those guys talk, boy, I'm telling you right now,

speaker: AS
words: they had a lot of high hopes and aspirations for the people in the log

speaker: AS
words: ging industry, you know. If they could put over some of the things that

speaker: AS
words: they said they was going to work for, why, you couldn't help but see it

speaker: AS
words: their way. And as far as signing up members was concerned, I think that

speaker: AS
words: pretty near a hundred percent of the people that worked in the logging

speaker: AS
words: camps carried their IWW card, but they never let anybody see it. They

words: didn't want anybody to know that they were IWWs. And of course, if it

words: come down to a showdown whether or not they would be any asset to the

speaker: AS
words: IWW, that's something that's questionable, you know. If it got down to

speaker: AS
words: the point where they was going to can everybody that belonged to the IWWs

speaker: AS
words: well, the majority would probably tear up their card or burn it, you know

speaker: AS
words: so that they wouldn't be identified as an IWW just to hold their job.

speaker: AS
words: But, nevertheless, down deep the seed was already sown, and these same

speaker: AS
words: men would have some bearing on any improvements in organized labor that

speaker: AS
words: they would have in the years to come.

speaker: SS
words: When you were at that meeting, was that the same time that they were

speaker: SS
words: fighting out in the camps?

speaker: AS
words: Oh, yeah.

speaker: SS
words: Do you remember anything else about it?

speaker: AS
words: Well, there was just a lot of speeches, you know. Different ones'd get

speaker: AS
words: upon the platform and make speeches as to —by golly, how they're gonna

speaker: AS
words: have to make improvements and this and that. You know, the whole country

speaker: AS
words: you take in the Midwest and the East, they were in quite a turmoil over

speaker: AS
words: organized labor too, you know, at that time and had been for a good many

speaker: AS
words: years before that. But you takejall the police force in Spokane and Seat

speaker: AS
words: tle and Portland, boy, they was right up on their toes. You take the City

speaker: AS
words: Fathers— it'd go from industry management to the City Fathers and to the

speaker: AS
words: police force and, boy, anybody that wanted to stick a knife in somebody

words: that was probably all right, but if they thought there was a Wobbly in

words: town they wanted him in jail!! Where he couldn't do any damage.

words: Why would it be that they never got a foothold in Potlatch, as compared

words: to the lumber camps? Do you have any thinking on why?

speaker: AS

words: good food, and in town pretty near everybody was living at home or living

words: with somebody. There was an awful lot of families in town that kept

speaker: AS
words: boarders. Well, those people had clean beds and they had good food.

speaker: AS
words: camps. See? About the only thing they had to fight for down here would

speaker: SS
words: out in the woods?

speaker: AS
words: Oh no. It all come together, because it was all one company, you know,

speaker: AS
words: as far as this company was concerned. You know if the company agreed to

speaker: AS
words: it, well then it was company-wide.

words: Do you know about when the eight hour day came in, roughly?

speaker: AS
words: Well, it was right at the tail end of World War I, I'd say along about

speaker: AS
words: 1919, somewhere around in there..

speaker: SS
words: there was a strike in the mill here.

words: Yeah, yeah. After they got— after the organized labor got a union going

words: here, and had been going for quite a little while, why they called a

words: strike here and I drove through that picket line down there every day

words: for — oh, I don't know how long it lasted— two or three weeks or such

words: a matter. And they had pickets across down here by th/$, railroad depot.

words: Of course, that's as close to the plant as they could get. But then, the

speaker: AS
words: pickets never bothered me because I wasn't a union member and I was just

words: part of the supervisory staff down there and they never attempted to stop

words: any of us going through. But anybody that just had an ordinary job—

speaker: AS
words: now then, the union was quite fair at that time I thought, because, the

speaker: AS
words: union they told the company that they would permit men in the power plant

speaker: AS
words: and all their guards, the watchmen, they would permit all of them to stay

speaker: AS
words: on the job, because they had to maintain fire protection. But then they

speaker: AS
words: did stop all production of lumber, although the supervisory staff, that

speaker: AS
words: is, I didn't get in on it, but then those that were in the lumber end of

speaker: AS
words: it, they processed some lumber and loaded some cars. But^the union stop

speaker: AS
words: ped 'em from pulling them cars out.

speaker: AS
words: So it really didn't do any good, you

speaker: AS
words: know, to load out any cars because they wouldn't let the train go in and

speaker: AS
words: get 'em. They were striking for more money, that's it was at

speaker: AS
words: the time; higher wages. And of course, whenever they did prevail and

speaker: AS
